lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Describing People & Places -- Relative Clauses (who / which / that)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Sandra Hayasaki | Aula 9 -- Describing People & Places

listenings.text: Let me tell you about my best friend. His name is Daniel, and he is the kind of person who never lets you down. He is the friend who calls you on your birthday and the one who remembers the small things. Daniel is very reliable; if he says he will be there at eight, he is there at eight. He is also incredibly generous. Last year, when I was moving to a new apartment, he was the only person who spent the whole weekend helping me, and he never asked for anything. The thing that I admire most about him is that he is the same with everyone. People who meet him once remember him for years. A friend who is both reliable and generous is a rare and wonderful thing.

I want to describe a place that I will never forget. A few years ago, I visited a small town which sits on the side of a green mountain. The center is a charming old square that is full of cafes and little shops. In the morning, it is a peaceful place where you can hear the birds and smell fresh bread. But in the evening, the square becomes crowded; it fills with families who walk together after dinner. From the top of the hill, there is a stunning view of the whole valley, and the light at sunset is something that I still remember today. It is not a famous city, but it is a place that feels like home the moment you arrive.

extra_audio.text: First, say who or what you want to describe. Then add a relative clause that gives the key detail, using who for a person or which for a thing. Next, add one more quality with another clause. After that, give a real example of that quality. Finally, say how that person or place makes you feel.
